title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The placebo effect is a fascinating psychological phenomenon (0) where a patient experiences a real improvement in their condition after receiving a dummy treatment. This occurs (9) because the individual’s belief in the cure triggers a physiological response. (10) Such is the power of the mind that even sham surgeries have been known to alleviate symptoms. Researchers have found that the effect is (11) far more complex than previously thought, involving neurochemical changes in the brain. (12) Although it was once dismissed as a mere illusion, it is now considered an essential component of clinical trials. (13) Not until scientists began using brain imaging did they truly understand how expectation modulates pain. (14) In spite of the ethical dilemmas it presents, the use of placebos remains a standard practice in medical research. (15) Unless a new drug can prove to be significantly more effective than a placebo, it will not be approved for public use. (16) Whether the effect can be harnessed for mainstream therapy is still being explored. This illustrates the profound connection between mental state and physical health, as our thoughts can physically alter our reality. It serves as a testament to the untapped potential of human belief.

example.text: The placebo effect is a fascinating psychological phenomenon (0) where a patient experiences a real improvement in their condition.
example.explanation: Relative adverb used to introduce a clause describing a situation.

questions.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce the reason for something.
Determiner used for emphasis followed by an inversion ('Such is...').
Adverb used to emphasize a comparative adjective ('far more complex').
Conjunction introducing a contrast or concession.
Part of the negative inversion 'Not until'.
Part of the fixed linker 'In spite of'.
Conjunction meaning 'if not', introducing a negative condition.
Conjunction used to introduce a doubt or a choice between possibilities.
